determination.primary_reasoning: Plan criteria not met; no regulatory carve-out applies; clinical standard does not strongly support surgery.

denial_specifies_criteria.reasoning: The denial notice explicitly identifies the clinical criteria applied: 'CC-SPINE-2024 (Plan Medical Policy: Cervical Spine Surgery)' and specifies the particular sections (§ 2.1, § 2.2, and § 2.3) used to evaluate the request. The notice then details how each criterion was applied to the clinical facts, satisfying the requirement to specify which clinical criteria from the plan policy were applied in reaching the denial decision.
cited_facts: case content

denial_specifies_clinical_reason.reasoning: The denial notice provides extensive clinical detail explaining which specific facts led to the denial: intact strength/reflexes/sensation without dermatomal distribution, negative Spurling's maneuver (leading to failure of § 2.1), only 4 weeks of physical therapy with patient discontinuation and only intermittent ibuprofen use without other agent classes or interventional procedures (leading to failure of § 2.2). This goes beyond merely stating which criteria were applied and identifies the specific clinical fact patterns that drove the determination.
cited_facts: case content (confidence 0.80)

denial_provides_imr_rights.reasoning: The denial notice contains a section titled 'RIGHT TO INDEPENDENT MEDICAL REVIEW' that explicitly informs the member of their right to request an Independent Medical Review under California Health & Safety Code § 1374.30 and § 1374.31, provides contact information for the California Department of Managed Health Care (phone and website), and states the 6-month filing deadline. This satisfies the condition requiring notice of IMR rights.

denial_factual_basis_correct.reasoning: The denial makes four substantive factual assertions: (1) PT was ~4 weeks and discontinued before completion, (2) only ibuprofen was trialed for pharmacotherapy, (3) no interventional procedures were attempted or contraindicated, and (4) neurological exam showed no radicular signs with negative Spurling's test and intact motor/sensory function. All four assertions are directly supported by the corresponding case record documents without contradiction.

cervical_radiculopathy_diagnosed.reasoning: The condition requires a confirmed diagnosis of cervical radiculopathy with nerve root compression and radicular symptoms (pain, numbness, weakness in dermatomal distribution). The clinical documentation explicitly states 'No radiculopathy documented,' the examination shows no dermatomal sensory loss, normal strength (5/5), negative Spurling's test, and the MRI shows no nerve root impingement. The documented pain is axial and shoulder girdle pain, not radicular symptoms in a dermatomal distribution.
cited_facts: [extract] case content (confidence 0.80): Chronic axial cervical pain with bilateral shoulder girdle pain. Mild multilevel cervical spondylosis (C4-C5, C5-C6, C6-C7 disc desiccation). No radiculopathy documented. No myelopathy documented. No disc herniation with neurological deficit documented.

[extract] case content (confidence 0.80): Examination dated 2026-03-18 by Dr. Elena Rodriguez: Mental status intact. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor: strength 5/5 throughout bilateral upper extremities including deltoid, biceps, triceps, wrist extension, finger flexion, intrinsic hand muscles. Sensation: intact to light touch, pinprick, and proprioception throughout C4-T1 distributions bilaterally. No dermatomal sensory loss documented. Reflexes: biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign. No clonus. Spurling's test negative bilaterally. Shoulder abduction relief sign negative. Range of motion: cervical flexion 50°, extension 40°, lateral rotation 70° bilaterally, lateral flexion 35° bilaterally with end-range discomfort. Tenderness to palpation over bilateral paraspinal muscles C5-T1 level and bilateral trapezii. Adson's maneuver negative.

[extract] case content (confidence 0.80): MRI cervical spine, 2026-01-14: Mild multilevel cervical spondylosis. C4-C5: mild disc desiccation, small posterior bulge, no significant central or foraminal stenosis. C5-C6: moderate disc desiccation, small broad-based posterior disc bulge measuring 1.5mm, no central stenosis, mild bilateral neural foraminal narrowing without nerve root impingement. C6-C7: mild disc desiccation, no significant bulge. No evidence of disc herniation, no central canal stenosis, no severe foraminal stenosis, no nerve root impingement, no cord compression, no myelomalacia signal. Impression: Mild multilevel cervical degenerative changes without significant neural compromise.

cervical_myelopathy_diagnosed.reasoning: The condition requires confirmed diagnosis of cervical myelopathy with specific myelopathic symptoms including spinal cord compression, gait disturbance, bilateral upper extremity weakness, hyperreflexia, positive Hoffman sign, and bowel/bladder dysfunction. The medical records explicitly state 'No myelopathy documented,' the MRI shows 'no cord compression,' the physical examination demonstrates normal strength (5/5), normal reflexes (2+), and negative Hoffman sign. None of the required myelopathic symptoms are present.

pt_contraindicated_or_futile.reasoning: The condition requires written documentation from the treating physician that physical therapy is contraindicated or unlikely to benefit the member. Dr. Rodriguez's documentation does not include any such attestation. In fact, the physical therapy record shows the patient improved during treatment (pain decreased from 7/10 to 4/10, improved range of motion) and self-discontinued after 4 weeks citing 'feeling better,' which indicates PT provided benefit rather than being contraindicated or unlikely to help.

[extract] case content (confidence 0.80): Physical therapy at Westside Rehabilitation...

functional_plateau_documented.reasoning: The condition requires both completion of a PT course AND documented functional plateau. The member did not complete the planned 6-week PT course (discontinued early by patient choice), and the treating PT explicitly documented continued expected progress with no plateau at time of discontinuation. The functional plateau definition requires documented failure to achieve meaningful improvement, but here the PT documented continued gains (15% ROM improvement, pain reduction from 7/10 to 4/10, 'making expected gains').
cited_facts: [extract] case content (confidence 0.80): Discharge summary, Westside Rehabilitation, 2025-09-02: Patient discontinued before completing planned 6-week course. Final session pain rating 4/10 (initial 7/10). Cervical range of motion improved by approximately 15% bilaterally. Patient declined further sessions. Discharge status: improvement noted, plan of care not completed.

[extract] case content (confidence 0.80): Progress notes consistent with discharge summary. No documentation of plateau, contraindication, or futility. Treating PT documented continued expected progress at time of discontinuation. Notes contain language: 'Patient making expected gains and would benefit from completion of planned care; however, patient elects to discontinue.'

structural_neurological_compromise.reasoning: The condition requires objective evidence of structural neurological compromise (e.g., cord compression with deficit, significant herniation causing radiculopathy, myelopathy). The MRI explicitly documents no cord compression, no nerve root impingement, no disc herniation, and no significant neural compromise. The physical examination shows 5/5 strength throughout, intact sensation, normal reflexes, negative Spurling's test, and no dermatomal sensory loss. The diagnosis confirms no radiculopathy, no myelopathy, and no disc herniation with neurological deficit. There is no structural neurological compromise present.
